cleaning_questions.q: Count total records in customers_raw.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_records
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Find distinct customer_id values.
Count duplicate customer_id entries.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS duplicate_count
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Find rows where first_name is empty or NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT *
FROM customers_raw
WHERE first_name IS NULL
   OR TRIM(first_name) = '';

cleaning_questions.q: Find rows where last_name is empty or NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT *
FROM customers_raw
WHERE last_name IS NULL
   OR TRIM(last_name) = '';

cleaning_questions.q: Count rows with NULL phone numbers.
Count customers per state.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT state, COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY state
ORDER BY customer_count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Find distinct customer_segment values.
Find customers where notes is empty or NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers_raw
WHERE notes IS NULL
   OR TRIM(notes) = '';

cleaning_questions.q: Count records where income contains letter K.
Count customers with NULL last_purchase_date.
Find the total number of customers per city.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY city
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: List all unique states in the dataset.
Count how many customers have notes.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS has_notes
FROM customers_raw
WHERE notes IS NOT NULL
  AND TRIM(notes) != '';

cleaning_questions.q: Show first 5 rows of customers_raw.
Count customers per customer_segment.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_segment, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_segment
ORDER BY total DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Show all column names and sample values.
Find the minimum and maximum customer_id.
Count customers with is_active as 'yes'.
Find customers registered in the year 2020.
Find duplicate rows (fully identical).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT *, COUNT(*) AS dup_count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, phone
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Find duplicate customer_id values.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Count total number of duplicate customers.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS duplicate_count
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove duplicate records keeping one copy.
Keep only the latest record for each duplicate customer_id.
Delete duplicates using ROW_NUMBER window function.
Identify duplicates based on email address.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Find duplicate phone numbers.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT phone, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
WHERE phone IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY phone
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Count total duplicate emails.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS dup_email_count
FROM (
  SELECT email
  FROM customers_raw
  GROUP BY email
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
) sub;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove duplicates using DISTINCT.
Create a clean table without duplicates.
cleaning_questions.sol:
CREATE TABLE customers_no_dupes AS
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id) *
FROM customers_raw
ORDER BY customer_id, ctid;

cleaning_questions.q: Find duplicates with same name AND email.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name, last_name, email, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY first_name, last_name, email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Detect partial duplicates (same customer_id, different data).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT email) AS emails, COUNT(*) AS rows
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers duplicated more than twice.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

cleaning_questions.q: Show all duplicate records ordered by customer_id.
Find duplicate state entries.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT state, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY state
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Identify duplicate addresses.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT address, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
WHERE address IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY address
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove duplicates using a CTE.
Find duplicates using GROUP BY + HAVING.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY cnt DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Keep only the first occurrence of each customer.
cleaning_questions.sol:
DELETE FROM customers_raw a
USING customers_raw b
WHERE a.ctid > b.ctid
  AND a.customer_id = b.customer_id;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove leading spaces from the notes column.
Remove trailing spaces from the notes column.
Convert email column to lowercase.
Convert first_name to proper case (Title Case).
Convert last_name to proper case (Title Case).
Remove extra spaces from the address column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(address), '\s+', ' ', 'g') AS clean_address
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace multiple consecutive spaces with a single space.
Extract the email domain from each email.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT email,
  SUBSTRING(email FROM POSITION('@' IN email) + 1) AS domain
FROM customers_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%';

cleaning_questions.q: Remove all special characters from phone numbers.
Replace dots with dashes in phone numbers.
Standardize phone numbers to (XXX) XXX-XXXX format.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET phone = '(' || SUBSTR(d,1,3) || ') ' || SUBSTR(d,4,3) || '-' || SUBSTR(d,7,4)
FROM (
  SELECT ctid,
    REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g') AS d
  FROM customers_raw
) sub
WHERE customers_raw.ctid = sub.ctid
  AND LENGTH(sub.d) = 10;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove all whitespace characters from email.
Trim leading and trailing spaces from customer_segment.
Convert state abbreviations to uppercase.
Convert city names to proper (Title) case.
Remove rows where notes is only blank spaces.
Replace all empty string fields with NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw SET
  first_name = NULLIF(TRIM(first_name), ''),
  last_name  = NULLIF(TRIM(last_name), ''),
  email      = NULLIF(TRIM(email), ''),
  phone      = NULLIF(TRIM(phone), ''),
  notes      = NULLIF(TRIM(notes), '');

cleaning_questions.q: Extract the area code from 10-digit phone numbers.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT phone,
  SUBSTRING(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g'), 1, 3) AS area_code
FROM customers_raw
WHERE LENGTH(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g')) = 10;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove all spaces from phone numbers.
Clean trailing spaces from 'Needs attention' in notes.
Standardize 'yes'/'Yes'/'YES' all to 'Yes' in is_active.
Fully normalize is_active to 'Yes' or 'No'.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET is_active = CASE
  WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('yes','true','y','1') THEN 'Yes'
  WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('no','false','n','0')  THEN 'No'
  ELSE NULL
END;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace 'Y' with 'Yes' in is_active column.
Replace 'N' with 'No' in is_active column.
Convert TRUE/FALSE string values to Yes/No.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET is_active = CASE
  WHEN LOWER(is_active) = 'true'  THEN 'Yes'
  WHEN LOWER(is_active) = 'false' THEN 'No'
  ELSE is_active
END;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace 'Invalid' in customer_segment with NULL.
List all non-standard is_active values.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT DISTINCT is_active, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY is_active
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Clean zip codes to 5-digit numeric format.
Remove newline characters from the address column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET address = REPLACE(REPLACE(address, E'\n', ' '), E'\r', ' ')
WHERE address LIKE '%
%' OR address LIKE '%%';

cleaning_questions.q: Remove double quotes from any text field.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET
  first_name = REPLACE(first_name, '"', ''),
  last_name  = REPLACE(last_name,  '"', ''),
  address    = REPLACE(address,    '"', '');

cleaning_questions.q: Convert birth_date VARCHAR to proper DATE format.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, birth_date,
  CASE
    WHEN birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
      THEN TO_DATE(birth_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    WHEN birth_date ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$'
      THEN TO_DATE(birth_date, 'MM/DD/YYYY')
    ELSE NULL
  END AS clean_birth_date
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Fix date in format 06/15/1990 to ISO format.
Detect the invalid date '1995-22-10' (month 22).
Convert registration_date to proper DATE type.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  TO_DATE(registration_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS reg_date
FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Convert last_purchase_date to DATE type.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  TO_DATE(last_purchase_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS purchase_date
FROM customers_raw
WHERE last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND last_purchase_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Find all records with dates not in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Replace all invalid birth_dates with NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET birth_date = NULL
WHERE birth_date IS NOT NULL
  AND (
    birth_date !~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
    OR SPLIT_PART(birth_date,'-',2)::INT > 12
  );

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers older than 60 using birth_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, first_name, birth_date,
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD'))) AS age
FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD'))) > 60;

cleaning_questions.q: Calculate each customer's age from birth_date.
Compare the stored age column vs calculated age from birth_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name,
  age::INT AS stored_age,
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')))::INT AS calc_age,
  CASE
    WHEN age::INT != EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')))::INT
    THEN 'MISMATCH' ELSE 'OK'
  END AS status
FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
  AND age ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Find records where stored age does not match calculated age.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT first_name, age::INT AS stored,
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')))::INT AS calc
  FROM customers_raw
  WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
    AND age ~ '^[0-9]+$'
) t WHERE stored != calc;

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers registered before year 2020.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date < '2020-01-01';

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers registered after year 2022.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date > '2022-12-31';

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers with a missing purchase date.
Calculate days since each customer's last purchase.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name, last_purchase_date,
  CURRENT_DATE - TO_DATE(last_purchase_date,'YYYY-MM-DD') AS days_since
FROM customers_raw
WHERE last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND last_purchase_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers who have been inactive for more than 1 year.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND TO_DATE(last_purchase_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')
      < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year';

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers who registered in year 2023.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date LIKE '2023%';

cleaning_questions.q: Extract the year from registration_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS reg_year
FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Extract the month from registration_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS reg_month
FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Extract the day from birth_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  EXTRACT(DAY FROM TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS birth_day
FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Convert all date columns to ISO 8601 format.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD'),'YYYY-MM-DD') AS iso_birth,
  TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD'),'YYYY-MM-DD') AS iso_reg
FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Replace invalid last_purchase_date with NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET last_purchase_date = NULL
WHERE last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND last_purchase_date !~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers who made a purchase BEFORE their registration.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND registration_date IS NOT NULL
  AND TO_DATE(last_purchase_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')
    < TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD');

cleaning_questions.q: Detect future dates (dates after today) in birth_date.
Fix inconsistent date formats in birth_date column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET birth_date = TO_CHAR(
  CASE
    WHEN birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')
    WHEN birth_date ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(birth_date,'MM/DD/YYYY')
    ELSE NULL
  END, 'YYYY-MM-DD'
);

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers born before 1980.
Count customers grouped by birth year.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS birth_year,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
GROUP BY birth_year
ORDER BY birth_year;

cleaning_questions.q: Find the youngest customer.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
ORDER BY TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD') DESC
LIMIT 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Find the oldest customer.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
ORDER BY TO_DATE(birth_date,'YYYY-MM-DD') ASC
LIMIT 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Calculate the average customer age.
Convert the income column from VARCHAR to NUMERIC.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN income ILIKE '%K'
      THEN (REPLACE(UPPER(income),'K',''))::NUMERIC * 1000
    WHEN income ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$'
      THEN income::NUMERIC
    ELSE NULL
  END AS income_numeric
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove the letter K from income values.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT income,
  REPLACE(UPPER(income), 'K', '') AS income_no_k
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ILIKE '%K';

cleaning_questions.q: Convert '120K' to 120000 numerically.
Replace negative income values with NULL.
Find and flag customers with income = 0.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE income = '0';

cleaning_questions.q: Convert total_purchases to integer type.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  CAST(total_purchases AS INTEGER) AS purchases_int
FROM customers_raw
WHERE total_purchases ~ '^-?[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Replace negative total_purchases with 0.
Replace NULL total_purchases with 0.
Calculate the average income (handling K suffix).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT ROUND(AVG(
  CASE
    WHEN income ILIKE '%K'
      THEN (REPLACE(UPPER(income),'K',''))::NUMERIC * 1000
    WHEN income ~ '^[0-9]+$'
      THEN income::NUMERIC
    ELSE NULL
  END
), 2) AS avg_income
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Find customers with numeric income greater than 100000.
Find the top 5 customers by income.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name, last_name, income
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$'
ORDER BY income::NUMERIC DESC
LIMIT 5;

cleaning_questions.q: Find the minimum income value.
Find the maximum income value.
Find the median income using PERCENTILE_CONT.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5)
  WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY income::NUMERIC) AS median_income
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Show average income grouped by customer_segment.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_segment,
  ROUND(AVG(income::NUMERIC), 2) AS avg_income
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$'
GROUP BY customer_segment
ORDER BY avg_income DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Find the average total_purchases per customer.
Find customers with more than 20 total purchases.
Find customers with zero total purchases.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE total_purchases = '0';

cleaning_questions.q: Calculate purchase frequency as purchases per year since registration.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name,
  total_purchases::NUMERIC /
  NULLIF(EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD'))),0) AS freq_per_year
FROM customers_raw
WHERE total_purchases ~ '^[0-9]+$'
  AND registration_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Calculate the sum of all total_purchases.
Normalize income values to a 0-1 scale.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id, income,
  ROUND(
    (income::NUMERIC - MIN(income::NUMERIC) OVER()) /
    NULLIF(MAX(income::NUMERIC) OVER() - MIN(income::NUMERIC) OVER(), 0)
  , 4) AS normalized_income
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Detect income outliers using standard deviation.
Replace non-numeric income values with NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET income = NULL
WHERE income IS NOT NULL
  AND income !~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$'
  AND income !~ '^[0-9]+K$';

cleaning_questions.q: Round all numeric income values to nearest 1000.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT income,
  ROUND(income::NUMERIC / 1000.0) * 1000 AS rounded_income
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Cast the age column to integer.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  CAST(age AS INTEGER) AS age_int
FROM customers_raw
WHERE age ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Detect invalid (non-numeric) values in total_purchases.
Handle NULL numeric fields using COALESCE.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(income,''), '0') AS income,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(total_purchases,''), '0') AS total_purchases
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace blank numeric fields with 0.
Detect unrealistic ages (below 0 or above 120).
Set age to NULL where it falls outside 18-100.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET age = NULL
WHERE age ~ '^[0-9]+$'
  AND (age::INT < 18 OR age::INT > 100);

cleaning_questions.q: Count the number of NULL values in every column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(customer_id)     AS null_id,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(first_name)      AS null_first,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(last_name)       AS null_last,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(email)           AS null_email,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(phone)           AS null_phone,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(income)          AS null_income,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(birth_date)      AS null_birth,
  COUNT(*)-COUNT(last_purchase_date) AS null_purchase
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace NULL phone numbers with 'Not Provided'.
Replace NULL total_purchases with 0.
Replace NULL or empty notes with 'No notes'.
Replace NULL income with the column average.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET income = (
  SELECT ROUND(AVG(income::NUMERIC))::TEXT
  FROM customers_raw
  WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$'
)
WHERE income IS NULL;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace missing city with 'Unknown'.
Replace missing state with 'Unknown'.
Replace missing last_purchase_date with a default date.
Replace NULL email with a placeholder.
Replace blank first/last names with NULL.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET
  first_name = NULLIF(TRIM(first_name), ''),
  last_name  = NULLIF(TRIM(last_name),  '');

cleaning_questions.q: Fill missing age column from birth_date.
Replace missing customer_segment with 'Regular'.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET customer_segment = 'Regular'
WHERE customer_segment IS NULL
   OR customer_segment = 'Invalid'
   OR TRIM(customer_segment) = '';

cleaning_questions.q: Replace NULL zip_code with '00000'.
Replace NULL address with 'Address Unknown'.
Replace NULL is_active with 'Unknown'.
Replace missing phone with a placeholder string.
Replace blank email values with NULL.
Fill multiple missing fields using COALESCE in a SELECT.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(first_name),''), 'Unknown')  AS first_name,
  COALESCE(phone,                  'Not Provided') AS phone,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(notes),''),  'No notes')    AS notes,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(city),''),   'Unknown')     AS city
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Replace NULL income with the median income.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET income = (
  SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5)
    WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY income::NUMERIC)
  FROM customers_raw WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$'
)::TEXT
WHERE income IS NULL;

cleaning_questions.q: Count rows that have more than 3 NULL fields.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS rows_with_3plus_nulls
FROM (
  SELECT customer_id,
    (CASE WHEN customer_id IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
     CASE WHEN first_name  IS NULL OR TRIM(first_name)='' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
     CASE WHEN email       IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
     CASE WHEN phone       IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
     CASE WHEN income      IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
     CASE WHEN birth_date  IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_count
  FROM customers_raw
) t WHERE null_count > 3;

cleaning_questions.q: Validate email format using a regex pattern.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE email !~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$';

cleaning_questions.q: Validate that phone numbers are exactly 10 digits.
Validate zip_code is a 5-digit number.
Validate age is between 18 and 100.
Validate that income is greater than 0.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE income IS NOT NULL
  AND income ~ '^-?[0-9]+$'
  AND income::NUMERIC <= 0;

cleaning_questions.q: Validate that total_purchases is >= 0.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE total_purchases IS NOT NULL
  AND total_purchases ~ '^-?[0-9]+$'
  AND total_purchases::INT < 0;

cleaning_questions.q: Validate is_active contains only allowed values.
Validate customer_segment is a known valid value.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_segment
FROM customers_raw
WHERE customer_segment NOT IN
  ('Regular','Premium','Gold','Platinum')
  AND customer_segment IS NOT NULL;

cleaning_questions.q: Detect invalid US state abbreviations.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE state NOT IN (
  'AL','AK','AZ','AR','CA','CO','CT','DE','FL','GA',
  'HI','ID','IL','IN','IA','KS','KY','LA','ME','MD',
  'MA','MI','MN','MS','MO','MT','NE','NV','NH','NJ',
  'NM','NY','NC','ND','OH','OK','OR','PA','RI','SC',
  'SD','TN','TX','UT','VT','VA','WA','WV','WI','WY'
);

cleaning_questions.q: Detect obviously fake city names.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT DISTINCT city FROM customers_raw
WHERE city ILIKE '%fake%'
   OR city ILIKE '%test%'
   OR city ILIKE '%unknown%'
   OR LENGTH(TRIM(city)) < 2;

cleaning_questions.q: Validate registration_date is before last_purchase_date.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE registration_date IS NOT NULL
  AND last_purchase_date IS NOT NULL
  AND TO_DATE(last_purchase_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')
    < TO_DATE(registration_date,'YYYY-MM-DD');

cleaning_questions.q: Validate that all email domains are legitimate.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT email,
  SUBSTRING(email FROM POSITION('@' IN email)+1) AS domain
FROM customers_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%'
  AND SUBSTRING(email FROM POSITION('@' IN email)+1)
      NOT LIKE '%.%';

cleaning_questions.q: Check phone format matches a standard pattern.
Validate birth_date is in the past.
Detect potentially fake addresses.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE address ILIKE '%fake%'
   OR address ILIKE '%test%'
   OR address ILIKE '%123 fake%';

cleaning_questions.q: Validate zip_code length is exactly 5.
Detect states that don't match the city.
cleaning_questions.sol:
-- Simplified: check city/state combos that appear inconsistently
SELECT city, state, COUNT(DISTINCT state) OVER (PARTITION BY city) AS state_count
FROM customers_raw
WHERE city IS NOT NULL;

cleaning_questions.q: Validate all numeric columns have valid numbers.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  CASE WHEN age ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN 'OK' ELSE 'INVALID' END AS age_valid,
  CASE WHEN income ~ '^[0-9]+$' OR income ILIKE '%K' THEN 'OK' ELSE 'INVALID' END AS income_valid,
  CASE WHEN total_purchases ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN 'OK' ELSE 'INVALID' END AS purchases_valid
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Detect completely empty records.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE TRIM(COALESCE(first_name,'')) = ''
  AND TRIM(COALESCE(last_name,''))  = ''
  AND TRIM(COALESCE(email,''))      = '';

cleaning_questions.q: Validate no duplicate emails exist after cleaning.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
WHERE email IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY LOWER(TRIM(email))
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

cleaning_questions.q: Detect inconsistent customer_segment capitalization.
Validate no emails are stored in uppercase.
Detect trailing spaces in any column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE first_name != RTRIM(COALESCE(first_name,''))
   OR last_name  != RTRIM(COALESCE(last_name,''))
   OR notes      != RTRIM(COALESCE(notes,''));

cleaning_questions.q: Detect rows where both first_name and last_name are blank.
Validate registration year is between 2015 and current year.
Create a fully cleaned customers table.
cleaning_questions.sol:
CREATE TABLE customers_clean AS
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id)
  customer_id,
  INITCAP(TRIM(first_name))       AS first_name,
  INITCAP(TRIM(last_name))        AS last_name,
  LOWER(TRIM(email))              AS email,
  REGEXP_REPLACE(COALESCE(phone,''),'[^0-9]','','g') AS phone,
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('yes','true','y','1') THEN 'Yes'
    ELSE 'No'
  END                             AS is_active,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(notes),''), 'No notes') AS notes
FROM customers_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%'
ORDER BY customer_id, ctid;

cleaning_questions.q: Standardize all phone numbers to uniform format.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw cr
SET phone = '(' || SUBSTR(d,1,3) || ') ' || SUBSTR(d,4,3) || '-' || SUBSTR(d,7,4)
FROM (
  SELECT ctid,
    REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g') AS d
  FROM customers_raw
) sub
WHERE cr.ctid = sub.ctid AND LENGTH(sub.d) = 10;

cleaning_questions.q: Standardize all email addresses to lowercase.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET email = LOWER(TRIM(email))
WHERE email IS NOT NULL
  AND email != LOWER(TRIM(email));

cleaning_questions.q: Create a full_name column combining first and last name.
Split the phone number into area code and local parts.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT phone,
  SUBSTR(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g'),1,3) AS area_code,
  SUBSTR(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g'),4,3) AS exchange,
  SUBSTR(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g'),7,4) AS line_num
FROM customers_raw
WHERE LENGTH(REGEXP_REPLACE(phone,'[^0-9]','','g')) = 10;

cleaning_questions.q: Split the address into street and city parts.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT address,
  SPLIT_PART(address, ',', 1) AS street,
  TRIM(SPLIT_PART(address, ',', 2)) AS city_part
FROM customers_raw
WHERE address LIKE '%,%';

cleaning_questions.q: Create an income_category column (Low/Medium/High).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name, income,
  CASE
    WHEN income::NUMERIC > 100000 THEN 'High'
    WHEN income::NUMERIC > 60000  THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'Low'
  END AS income_category
FROM customers_raw
WHERE income ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Create an age_group column (Youth/Adult/Senior).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT first_name, age,
  CASE
    WHEN age::INT < 30 THEN 'Youth'
    WHEN age::INT < 60 THEN 'Adult'
    ELSE 'Senior'
  END AS age_group
FROM customers_raw
WHERE age ~ '^[0-9]+$';

cleaning_questions.q: Flag invalid records with a has_error boolean column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  (
    email NOT LIKE '%@%'
    OR (age ~ '^[0-9]+$' AND (age::INT < 0 OR age::INT > 120))
    OR (income ~ '^-[0-9]+$')
    OR (total_purchases ~ '^-[0-9]+$')
  ) AS has_error
FROM customers_raw;

cleaning_questions.q: Remove records that have more than 4 data errors.
cleaning_questions.sol:
DELETE FROM customers_raw
WHERE customer_id IN (
  SELECT customer_id FROM (
    SELECT customer_id,
      (CASE WHEN email NOT LIKE '%@%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
       CASE WHEN first_name IS NULL OR TRIM(first_name)='' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
       CASE WHEN income ~ '^-' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
       CASE WHEN total_purchases ~ '^-' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
       CASE WHEN phone IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS errors
    FROM customers_raw
  ) t WHERE errors > 4
);

cleaning_questions.q: Create a cleaned is_active boolean column.
Create normalized segment values column.
cleaning_questions.sol:
ALTER TABLE customers_raw ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS segment_clean VARCHAR(50);
UPDATE customers_raw
SET segment_clean = CASE
  WHEN customer_segment IN ('Regular','Premium','Gold','Platinum')
    THEN customer_segment
  ELSE 'Regular'
END;

cleaning_questions.q: Build a data quality score (0-5) for each customer.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  (
    CASE WHEN email LIKE '%@%.%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
    CASE WHEN phone IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
    CASE WHEN birth_date ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
    CASE WHEN income ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
    CASE WHEN first_name IS NOT NULL AND TRIM(first_name) != '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
  ) AS quality_score
FROM customers_raw
ORDER BY quality_score DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Identify incomplete records (missing 2+ core fields).
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE (
  CASE WHEN first_name IS NULL OR TRIM(first_name)='' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
  CASE WHEN email      IS NULL OR email NOT LIKE '%@%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
  CASE WHEN phone      IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END +
  CASE WHEN income     IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END
) >= 2;

cleaning_questions.q: Create an audit log table for cleaning operations.
cleaning_questions.sol:
CREATE TABLE cleaning_audit (
  audit_id   SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  table_name VARCHAR(100),
  operation  VARCHAR(50),
  rows_affected INT,
  performed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  notes TEXT
);
INSERT INTO cleaning_audit(table_name,operation,rows_affected,notes)
VALUES ('customers_raw','standardize_email',
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM customers_raw WHERE email != LOWER(email)),
  'Converted emails to lowercase');

cleaning_questions.q: Standardize all zip_code values to 5-digit format.
cleaning_questions.sol:
UPDATE customers_raw
SET zip_code = LPAD(
  REGEXP_REPLACE(zip_code,'[^0-9]','','g'),
  5, '0'
)
WHERE zip_code IS NOT NULL
  AND LENGTH(REGEXP_REPLACE(zip_code,'[^0-9]','','g')) <= 5;

cleaning_questions.q: Detect inconsistent customer_segment values.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_segment, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_segment
ORDER BY count DESC;

cleaning_questions.q: Merge duplicate customers by combining their data.
cleaning_questions.sol:
INSERT INTO customers_clean
SELECT
  customer_id,
  MAX(first_name) AS first_name,
  MAX(last_name)  AS last_name,
  MIN(email)      AS email,
  MAX(phone)      AS phone,
  MAX(income)     AS income,
  MIN(registration_date) AS registration_date,
  MAX(last_purchase_date) AS last_purchase_date,
  SUM(COALESCE(total_purchases::INT,0)) AS total_purchases
FROM customers_raw
GROUP BY customer_id;

cleaning_questions.q: Create a VIEW of the cleaned dataset.
cleaning_questions.sol:
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW v_customers_clean AS
SELECT
  customer_id,
  INITCAP(TRIM(first_name)) AS first_name,
  INITCAP(TRIM(last_name))  AS last_name,
  LOWER(TRIM(email))        AS email,
  REGEXP_REPLACE(COALESCE(phone,''),'[^0-9]','','g') AS phone_digits,
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('yes','true','y','1') THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE
  END AS is_active,
  COALESCE(NULLIF(TRIM(notes),''),'No notes') AS notes
FROM customers_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%';

cleaning_questions.q: Build a complete step-by-step data cleaning pipeline.
cleaning_questions.sol:
-- STEP 1: Trim whitespace
UPDATE customers_raw SET
  first_name = TRIM(first_name),
  last_name  = TRIM(last_name),
  email      = LOWER(TRIM(email));

-- STEP 2: Normalize is_active
UPDATE customers_raw SET is_active = CASE
  WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('yes','true','y','1') THEN 'Yes'
  ELSE 'No'
END;

-- STEP 3: Fix income K values
UPDATE customers_raw
SET income = ((REPLACE(UPPER(income),'K',''))::NUMERIC*1000)::TEXT
WHERE income ILIKE '%K';

-- STEP 4: Remove duplicates
DELETE FROM customers_raw
WHERE ctid NOT IN (SELECT MIN(ctid) FROM customers_raw GROUP BY customer_id);

-- STEP 5: Create clean table
CREATE TABLE customers_final AS
SELECT * FROM customers_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%';

cleaning_questions.q: Track which records were corrected during cleaning.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT customer_id,
  CASE WHEN email != LOWER(email) THEN 'email ' ELSE '' END ||
  CASE WHEN first_name != INITCAP(first_name) THEN 'name ' ELSE '' END ||
  CASE WHEN income ILIKE '%K' THEN 'income ' ELSE '' END AS corrections_needed
FROM customers_raw
WHERE
  email != LOWER(email)
  OR first_name != INITCAP(COALESCE(first_name,''))
  OR income ILIKE '%K';

cleaning_questions.q: Build a staging table for cleaned data.
cleaning_questions.sol:
CREATE TABLE customers_staging (
  LIKE customers_raw INCLUDING ALL
);
INSERT INTO customers_staging
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id)
  customer_id,
  INITCAP(TRIM(first_name)),
  INITCAP(TRIM(last_name)),
  LOWER(TRIM(email)),
  REGEXP_REPLACE(COALESCE(phone,''),'[^0-9]','','g'),
  birth_date, age, income, address, city,
  UPPER(state), zip_code, registration_date,
  last_purchase_date, total_purchases,
  customer_segment, is_active, notes
FROM customers_raw
ORDER BY customer_id, ctid;

cleaning_questions.q: Create an automated data validation query.
cleaning_questions.sol:
SELECT
  'total_rows'           AS check_name, COUNT(*)::TEXT AS result FROM customers_raw
UNION ALL
SELECT 'null_emails',   COUNT(*)::TEXT FROM customers_raw WHERE email IS NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT 'invalid_emails',COUNT(*)::TEXT FROM customers_raw WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@%'
UNION ALL
SELECT 'duplicates',    (COUNT(*)-COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id))::TEXT FROM customers_raw
UNION ALL
SELECT 'neg_income',    COUNT(*)::TEXT FROM customers_raw WHERE income ~ '^-'
UNION ALL
SELECT 'null_phones',   COUNT(*)::TEXT FROM customers_raw WHERE phone IS NULL;

cleaning_questions.q: Build a full ETL cleaning script.
cleaning_questions.sol:
-- ETL: Extract → Transform → Load

-- EXTRACT: raw source
CREATE TEMP TABLE etl_raw AS SELECT * FROM customers_raw;

-- TRANSFORM: clean all fields
CREATE TEMP TABLE etl_clean AS
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id)
  customer_id,
  INITCAP(NULLIF(TRIM(first_name),''))  AS first_name,
  INITCAP(NULLIF(TRIM(last_name),''))   AS last_name,
  LOWER(NULLIF(TRIM(email),''))         AS email,
  REGEXP_REPLACE(COALESCE(phone,''),'[^0-9]','','g') AS phone,
  CASE WHEN income ILIKE '%K'
    THEN ((REPLACE(UPPER(income),'K',''))::NUMERIC*1000)::TEXT
    ELSE NULLIF(REGEXP_REPLACE(income,'[^0-9.]','','g'),'')
  END AS income,
  CASE WHEN LOWER(is_active) IN ('yes','true','y','1') THEN 'Yes' ELSE 'No' END AS is_active
FROM etl_raw
WHERE email LIKE '%@%'
ORDER BY customer_id, ctid;

-- LOAD
INSERT INTO customers_final SELECT * FROM etl_clean;

cleaning_questions.q: Export the clean dataset to CSV.
cleaning_questions.sol:
COPY (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    INITCAP(TRIM(first_name))  AS first_name,
    INITCAP(TRIM(last_name))   AS last_name,
    LOWER(TRIM(email))         AS email,
    REGEXP_REPLACE(COALESCE(phone,''),'[^0-9]','','g') AS phone,
    customer_segment, is_active
  FROM customers_raw
  WHERE email LIKE '%@%'
  ORDER BY customer_id
) TO '/tmp/customers_clean.csv'
WITH (FORMAT CSV, HEADER TRUE, DELIMITER ',', QUOTE '"');

join_questions.q: Write a query to display employee name and department name.
Write a query to show all employees with their department names.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
ORDER BY e.emp_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to list employees who do not belong to any department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id IS NULL;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments that have no employees.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
WHERE e.emp_id IS NULL;

join_questions.q: Write a query to display employee name, salary, and department name.
Write a query to find employees working in the 'IT' department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_name = 'IT';

join_questions.q: Write a query to list all departments and employees (including unmatched records).
Write a query to show employees along with department location.
Write a query to find employees whose department is located in Delhi.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.location = 'Delhi';

join_questions.q: Write a query to display employee names sorted by department name.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
ORDER BY d.dept_name, e.emp_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to count employees in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS emp_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
ORDER BY emp_count DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find the department with the maximum number of employees.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS emp_count
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
ORDER BY emp_count DESC
LIMIT 1;

join_questions.q: Write a query to calculate average salary in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, ROUND(AVG(e.salary),2) AS avg_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find total salary paid in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, SUM(e.salary) AS total_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
ORDER BY total_salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments where average salary is greater than 50000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, ROUND(AVG(e.salary),2) AS avg_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
HAVING AVG(e.salary) > 50000;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find the highest salary in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, MAX(e.salary) AS max_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find the lowest salary in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, MIN(e.salary) AS min_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to count employees in each department location.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.location, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS emp_count
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.location
ORDER BY emp_count DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments with more than 5 employees.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, COUNT(e.emp_id) AS emp_count
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
HAVING COUNT(e.emp_id) > 5;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find the department with the lowest average salary.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, ROUND(AVG(e.salary),2) AS avg_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
ORDER BY avg_salary ASC
LIMIT 1;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees earning more than 60000 along with their department names.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > 60000
ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees whose salary is greater than the average salary of their department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > (
  SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
  FROM employees e2
  WHERE e2.dept_id = e.dept_id
)
ORDER BY d.dept_name, e.salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees working in departments located in Mumbai.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.location = 'Mumbai';

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees whose department name starts with 'S'.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_name LIKE 'S%';

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who work in either HR or IT department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_name IN ('HR', 'IT')
ORDER BY d.dept_name, e.emp_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to display employees and their managers.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name AS employee,
       COALESCE(m.emp_name, 'No Manager') AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who do not have a manager.
Write a query to find managers and the number of employees reporting to them.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT m.emp_name AS manager,
       COUNT(e.emp_id) AS total_reports
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id
GROUP BY m.emp_name
ORDER BY total_reports DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who report to the same manager.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e1.emp_name AS employee1,
       e2.emp_name AS employee2,
       m.emp_name AS common_manager
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.manager_id = e2.manager_id
  AND e1.emp_id < e2.emp_id
JOIN employees m ON e1.manager_id = m.emp_id;

join_questions.q: Write a query to display employee name and manager name along with department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name,
       COALESCE(m.emp_name,'No Manager') AS manager_name,
       d.dept_name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find the second highest salary in each department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT emp_name, salary, dept_name
FROM (
  SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY e.dept_id
      ORDER BY e.salary DESC
    ) AS dr
  FROM employees e
  JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
) ranked
WHERE dr = 2;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who earn the highest salary in their department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary = (
  SELECT MAX(e2.salary)
  FROM employees e2
  WHERE e2.dept_id = e.dept_id
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who work in departments with more than 10 employees.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id IN (
  SELECT dept_id
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees whose salary is greater than their manager's salary.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary,
       m.emp_name AS manager_name, m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who work in the same department as their manager.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.emp_id
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.dept_id = m.dept_id;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees and department where salary > 50000 and location = Delhi.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name, d.location
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > 50000
  AND d.location = 'Delhi'
ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees hired in departments located in Bangalore.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.hire_date, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.location = 'Bangalore'
ORDER BY e.hire_date;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees and department ordered by salary descending.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees working in departments with average salary above 70000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id IN (
  SELECT dept_id
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  HAVING AVG(salary) > 70000
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments where total salary expense exceeds 500000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name, SUM(e.salary) AS total_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_name
HAVING SUM(e.salary) > 500000
ORDER BY total_salary DESC;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who are the only employee in their department.
Write a query to find employees who share the same salary in the same department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e1.emp_name, e2.emp_name AS colleague,
       e1.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.dept_id = e2.dept_id
  AND e1.salary = e2.salary
  AND e1.emp_id < e2.emp_id
JOIN departments d ON e1.dept_id = d.dept_id;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees working in departments with the highest average salary.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id = (
  SELECT dept_id FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  ORDER BY AVG(salary) DESC
  LIMIT 1
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments where every employee earns more than 50000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT d.dept_name
FROM departments d
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM employees e
  WHERE e.dept_id = d.dept_id
    AND e.salary <= 50000
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who work in the department with the highest number of employees.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id = (
  SELECT dept_id FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC
  LIMIT 1
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who work in departments where no employee earns less than 30000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id IN (
  SELECT dept_id FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  HAVING MIN(salary) >= 30000
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees who earn the second highest salary overall but show department name.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary = (
  SELECT DISTINCT salary
  FROM employees
  ORDER BY salary DESC
  OFFSET 1 LIMIT 1
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find departments that have employees with salaries greater than 100000.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT DISTINCT d.dept_name
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > 100000
ORDER BY d.dept_name;

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees whose salary is equal to the average salary of their department.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary = (
  SELECT ROUND(AVG(e2.salary))
  FROM employees e2
  WHERE e2.dept_id = e.dept_id
);

join_questions.q: Write a query to find employees whose department has the highest total salary expense.
join_questions.sol:
SELECT e.emp_name, e.salary, d.dept_name
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE d.dept_id = (
  SELECT dept_id FROM employees
  GROUP BY dept_id
  ORDER BY SUM(salary) DESC
  LIMIT 1
);

interview_questions.q: Find customers with purchases in 3 consecutive months.
Find the 2nd highest income customer in each segment.
Find users who logged in on Jan 1 AND Jan 2.
Find departments where avg salary > company average.
Find the longest streak of daily logins.
Find all pairs of customers in the same city.
Deduplicate a transaction table keeping the latest record.
Find customers who placed an order every month this year.
Calculate the 7-day rolling average of daily signups.
Find all transactions where amount > avg of the same user.
